Make a Measurement Maths Trail In this unit students first go round a maths trail devised by the teacher. They then work in groups to devise and complete a maths trail of their own. They then go round this new maths trail or, if another class can work in parallel, each class goes round the other's trail. carry out practical measuring tasks using appropriate metric units. make measurement estimates using appropriate metric units. pose measurement questions. The maths trail can be made to accommodate any level of measurement expertise and any range of measurement topics. To make a maths trail (which can be inside the classroom or school buildings or outside, or a combination of both), the steps are: - Select 4 to 10 'stations' to form a route round the 'trail'. At each station students will have one or more questions to answer on a worksheet. - Make up questions for each station or site, which can ONLY be answered at that site. - Either have the questions fixed at each station, OR have all questions included on the worksheet with space for answers. - Print copies for each group. Comments on questions - Use the questions to practice or consolidate aspects of Measurement appropriate to the class - Make sure the questions could only be answered while at the Station. - Use a variety of types of question: closed and open; exact measurements and estimations; one-word answers and questions needing explanations; cover a range of types of measurement - Make sure questions do not need elaborate equipment; for example each group could take only pencil, worksheet, a tape measure or ruler, and a calculator. - Make sure you have all the answers! - Make sure questions do not refer to objects which might be removed from the station. - Write a clear description of the site of each station on the worksheet, and preferably have signs (Station 1) fixed at each site. - The Maths Trail should be easy to complete within one lesson. Suggestions for Measurement Questions (Of course, questions do not need to be all measurement) - Stand at the station. Write your estimate of the distance in metres to the edge of the grass. Now measure and record your measurement. How good was your estimate? - Estimate the height of the tree to your left. Explain your estimation strategy. - Draw and name an object about ten metres from the station. - How high is each brick in the wall on your right. Use this to calculate the height of the wall. - Estimate the capacity in litres of the (bin) by the door. Explain your strategy. - Calculate the area of the door/window. - Name an object you can see which is about one cubic metre in volume. - How long in seconds do you think it will take you to walk from the Station to the tree and back? Write your estimate, then each member of the group in turn walk while other members time the walk. - Measure and record the diameter and circumference of the circular window/ flower tub. Write what you decide from your measurements. - Estimate the area of the (irregular) flowerbed. Explain your estimation strategy. - Estimate the weight of the flower tub. Session 1 (Mark out the Maths Trail in advance) - Explain how a Maths Trail works. - Arrange groups of 2 - 4 students. - Assign groups a station to start from to avoid congestion. Check that all groups have (only) the agreed materials and the worksheet. - Set a time limit for completion of the Maths Trail. - Students move round the stations in sequence, completing the worksheets as they go. - Discussion of Maths Trail. - As a class discuss each question in turn. Compare answers and solution strategies. Which questions were most difficult? Which questions were most interesting? Were any questions too easy? - Note skills and knowledge that need consolidating and revise these with the class. Planning a Class Maths Trail. - Explain that the class in groups is going to plan and prepare a new Maths Trail. - Brainstorm with the class types of questions that could be asked about length, area, capacity, mass, volume, and time. - Stress that questions must only be able to be answered using data at the site. - To practice question setting, assign each group to a part of the classroom and have them devise and write two appropriate questions, together with answers. - Assign two different types of measurement (length, area ) to each group. Each group could be asked to produce one open and one closed question. - Come together to discuss the questions, especially drawing out strengths and weaknesses. - It is important that groups realise that making a good question is not a simple or quick exercise. Encourage interesting and varied questions. - Assign stations, types of measurement and types of questions to each group. - As each group prepares questions, discuss them and suggest where changes are needed. - Groups write up (or word process) their questions (each on half an A4 sheet) including space for answers, and provide their answers on a different piece of paper. - The questions then need putting together and copying for the next session. Groups complete the Maths Trail as in Session 1, then come together to discuss answers and strategies and to comment on the idea of a Maths Trail. Some students may wish to make a new Maths Trail for a younger class to complete, which may include number and space. We'll get something different, these are good. 'Kay I'll get you a snow cone. An adverb is a word used to tell more about a verb, and it almost always answers the questions "how?", "when?", "where?", "how often?", and "in what way?". Words like "slowly", "loudly", "carefully", "quickly", "quietly" or "sadly" are all adverbs. Adverbs usually, but not always, end in "-ly". First, you'll need to make a set of ABC circles: - Trace circles on construction paper using a small cup (mine are about 2 1/2" big) - Cut them out - Write all the Letters on the circles. (I made a set of Upper-case Letters, and a set of Lower-case Letters.) Second, make a set of ABC cones: - Using brown paper, cut out 26 cone shapes. - On one side of the cones write the alphabet in upper-case letters. On the other side write the lower-case letters. Third, you PLAY! - Lay out all the letter circles on the floor or table. - Lay out all the cones, OR one cone at a time and have the kids make "ice cream cones" by matching up the letters. - They can match upper-case to upper-case, or lower-case to lower case: - OR you can have them match lower-case to upper-case: So many fun possibilities! Did it go up in the air? Many years ago in a small Indian village, A farmer had the misfortune Of owing a large sum of money to a village moneylender. The Moneylender, who was old and ugly, fancied the farmer's beautiful Daughter. So he proposed a bargain. He said he would forgo the farmer's debt if he could marry his Daughter. Both the farmer and his daughter were horrified by the Proposal. So the cunning money-lender suggested that they let Providence decide the matter. He told them that he would put a black Pebble and a white pebble into an empty money bag. Then the girl would Have to pick one pebble from the bag. 1) If she picked the black pebble, she would become his wife and her father's debt would be forgiven. 2) If she picked the white pebble she need not marry him and her father's debt would still be forgiven. 3) But if she refused to pick a pebble, her father would be thrown into Jail. They were standing on a pebble strewn path in the farmer's field. As They talked, the moneylender bent over to pick up two pebbles. As he Picked them up, the sharp-eyed girl noticed that he had picked up two Black pebbles and put them into the bag. He then asked the girl to pick A pebble from the bag. Now, imagine that you were standing in the field. What would you have Done if you were the girl? If you had to advise her, what would you Have told her? Careful analysis would produce three possibilities: 1. The girl should refuse to take a pebble. 2. The girl should show that there were two black pebbles in the bag And expose the money-lender as a cheat. 3. The girl should pick a black pebble and sacrifice herself in order To save her father from his debt and imprisonment. Take a moment to ponder over the story. The above story is used with The hope that it will make us appreciate the difference between lateral And logical thinking. The girl's dilemma cannot be solved with Traditional logical thinking. Think of the consequences if she chooses The above logical answers. What would you recommend to the Girl to do? Well, here is what she did: The girl put her hand into the moneybag and drew out a pebble. A teacher teaching Maths to seven-year-old Arnav asked him, "If I give you one apple and one apple and one apple, how many apples will you have? "Within a few seconds Arnav replied confidently, "Four!" The dismayed teacher was expecting an effortless correct answer (three). She was disappointed. "Maybe the child did not listen properly," she thought. She repeated, "Arnav, listen carefully.. If I give you one apple and one apple and one apple, how many apples will you have?" Arnav had seen the disappointment on his teacher's face. He calculated again on his fingers. But within him he was also searching for the answer that will make the teacher happy. His search for the answer was not for the correct one, but the one that will make his teacher happy. This time hesitatingly he replied, "Four." The disappointment stayed on the teacher's face. She remembered that Arnav liked strawberries. She thought maybe he doesn't like apples and that is making him loose focus. This time with an exaggerated excitement and twinkling in her eyes she asked, "If I give you one strawberry and one strawberry and one strawberry, then how many you will have?" Seeing the teacher happy, young Arnav calculated on his fingers again. There was no pressure on him, but a little on the teacher. She wanted her new approach to succeed. With a hesitating smile young Arnav enquired, The teacher now had a victorious smile. Her approach had succeeded. She wanted to congratulate herself. But one last thing remained. Once again she asked him, "Now if I give you one apple and one apple and one more apple how many will you have?" Promptly Arnav answered, "Four!" The teacher was aghast. "How Arnav, how?" she demanded in a little stern and irritated voice. In a voice that was low and hesitating young Arnav replied, "Because I already have one apple in my bag." As a man walked a desolate beach one cold, gray morning he began to see another figure, far in the distance. Slowly the two approached each other, and he could make out a local native who kept leaning down, picking something up and throwing it out into the water. Time and again he hurled things into the ocean. As the distance between them continued to narrow, the man could see that the native was picking up starfish that had been washed upon the beach and, one at a time, was throwing them back into the water. Puzzled, the man approached the native and asked what he was doing. "I'm throwing these starfish back into the ocean. You see, it's low tide right now and all of these starfish have been washed up onto the shore. If I don't throw them back into the sea, they'll die up here from lack of oxygen." "But there must be thousands of starfish on this beach," the man replied. "You can't possibly get to all of them. There are just too many. And this same thing is probably happening on hundreds of beaches all up and down this coast. Can't you see that you can't possibly make a difference?" The local native smiled, bent down and picked up another starfish, and as he threw it back into the sea he replied, "Made a difference to that one!" Moral of the story is:-- Each of us is but one person: limited, burdened with our own cares and responsibilities. We may feel there is so much to be done, and we have so little to give. We're usually short of everything, especially time and money. When we leave this shore, there will still be millions of starfish stranded on the beach. Maybe we can't change the whole world, but there isn't one of us who can't help change one person's whole world. One at a time. We can make a difference. [aft leaves kitchen and crawls back to sofa to dial phone] [opens the airplane coloring book and points to a pilot's hat] Title: How to Edit Business Information on Yelp.com Editing your business information on Yelp is crucial to ensure that potential customers have access to accurate and up-to-date details about your products or services. This tutorial will guide you through the process step by step so that you can effectively manage your business profile on Yelp. **Step 1:** **Log into Your Yelp Business Account** To begin editing your business information on Yelp, first log into your account at <https://biz.yelp.com/> using your email address and password associated with your Yelp Business Account. If you don't yet have an account, sign up for one as this grants you access to edit and manage your business listing. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Make sure to remember the login credentials for future reference when updating other aspects of your business profile. * Having trouble logging in? Try resetting your password via the 'Forgot Password?' link on the login page. **Step 2:** **Access Your Business Profile** Once logged in, click on your user icon located at the top right corner of the screen, then select 'My Claimed Businesses' from the dropdown menu. You will be directed to a list of businesses claimed under your account; find your specific business and click its name to proceed. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Keep track of all businesses claimed under your account to avoid confusion while managing multiple locations. * Use filters like 'All', 'Unclaimed', 'Claimed', and 'Action Required' available above the listed businesses to efficiently navigate between them. **Step 3:** **Navigate to Business Information Section** From your claimed business dashboard, look for the left sidebar navigation panel. Under the category 'Business Info', click on the option labeled 'Overview'. Here, you'll see various sections related to your business info including Name, Address, Phone Number, Categories, Website URL, etc., which can be edited individually. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Double check every piece of information before making changes since incorrect data may mislead potential clients. * Consistency across online platforms enhances local SEO (Search Engine Optimization) performance - make sure your NAP (Name, Address, Phone number) are uniform everywhere. **Step 4:** **Update Business Details Accordingly** Now comes the part where you actually modify the necessary fields within the Overview section based on what needs changing. Be thorough but concise with descriptions, provide correct contact numbers, specify appropriate categories, include relevant keywords, and input proper timings if applicable. Remember to save any updates made. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Refrain from adding unnecessary keywords just for ranking purposes - overuse could lead to penalties instead. * Update hours accurately especially during holidays or special events to prevent customer disappointment. **Step 5:** **Review Changes Made** After saving the modifications, review everything carefully once more to confirm their accuracy. Check whether they appear correctly both on desktop and mobile versions of Yelp. Report any discrepancies immediately. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Regular reviews help maintain accurate profiles - consider setting reminders quarterly or bi-annually. * Encourage satisfied customers to leave positive feedback and respond politely to negative ones to build reputation. By following these steps diligently, you can successfully manage and update your business information on Yelp.com, ensuring that potential customers always receive accurate and current details about your offerings. I think the earlier ones he was more stuck on certain things and did not want to transition. Section 1--The major and minor scales Hundreds of years ago, cassical composers used the "modal" system. Dorian, Hypodorian, Phrygian, Hypophrygian, Lydian, Hypolydian, Mixolydian, Hypomixolydian, Aeolian, Hypoaeolian, and Ionian were the modes used back then and music was primarily based around the use of scales. At the dawn of "tonal" music (music theory still used today to analyze and create music) composers such as Bach and theorists such a Jean-Phillipe Rameau began to narrow this down to simply Ionian and Aeolian, and renamed them the major and mino scales. Major scale: 1-2-3-4-5-6-7 or WWhWWWh* Minor scale: 1-2-b3-4-5-b6-b7 or WhWWhWW *If I haven't already explained this in previous lesons, a "whole step" or a tone is the distance between 2 notes where there is one in-between. From C to D is a whole step up. From C to Bb is a whole step down. From F to G is a whole step up. From A to G is a whole step down. And so on. A half step or semitone is the distance between 2 notes right next to each other. B to C is a half step up. D to Eb is a half step up. And so on. Sometimes you'll see scales spelled out by their whole-step/half-step pattern. Let's construct a Cmajor scale. C is our tonic, 1, so we go up a whole step to 2, D, up another whole step to 3, E, up a half step to 4, F, up a whole step to 5, G, up a whole step to 6, A, up a whole step to 7, B, and up a final half step to the octave 8, C. Let's construct an Eminor scale. Start with E, 1, go up a whole step to , F#, go up a half step to b3, G, go up a whole step to 4, A, go up a whole step to 5, B, go up a half step to b6, C, go up a whole step to b7, D, and go up a final whole step to the octave 8, E. When writing modern music, we start with a key. Homework: before moving on watch the musictheory.net videos under the category "Scales and Key Signatures." Let's say we're in the key of Gmajor. The Gmajor scale is made of the notes G-A-B-C-D-E-F#. This doesn't mean we can't use notes like C# or F or Bb. We can still use any note we want, but the thing that makes it the key of Gmajor is that your harmony (chord progression) will eventually "resolve" to the Gmajor chord, or will feel like it wants to resolve there. What is resolution in music? When you play the chord progression Bminor, Emajor, Amajor, there is a feeling that the Amajor chord is a sort of home base. It's something you just hear as a musician. This is where the song wants to end, but there are other interesting things you can do. Just because the song WANTS to end on Amajor, doesn't mean it has to. If you ended the song on Fminor, even though the key of the song is Amajor, it might create an interesting effect. Back to our Gmajor song...The key signature (which you should know from the videos) will show you that there is an F#. So when writing the sheet music, instead of writing the sharp sign (#) every time you want to play an F#, you just write the note where the F is and the reader already knows that it is sharped. If you want to play an F natural, you'll have to put the natural sign in front of the F (the sign that looks like a square). Just remember, if you put that natural sign there, it means that all the Fs throughout the song are natural, until you put the sharp or flat sign there again (# or b). You can also alter all of the other notes. If you want to use a C# instead of a C, just use the sharp sign, but remember that the C will stay sharp for the rest of the song unless you put a natural sign there again. Section 3--Diatonic Harmony Diatonic harmony means that all of the notes for all of your chords fit a diatonic scale. A diatonic scale is a seven-note scale comprising five whole steps and two half steps, in which the two half steps are separated from each other by either two or three whole steps. The major scale and the minor scale are diatonic scales. So how do we make sure all of our chords are diatonic? Let's start with the Dmajor scale. Now we stack "generic 3rds" onto each note. A generic 3rd is a 3rd when we don't know yet if it's a major or minor 3rd (3 or b3). Check the videos on musictheory.net under "intervals." The 3rd of D is some kind of an F. The 3rd of E is some kind of a G. Work all this out and you end up with: Now we have to turn these "generic intervals" into "specific intervals" which match the key signature. According to the key signature, all notes are natural except for C and F, which are both sharp. So we go back to those 3rds and make sure all of the Cs and Fs are shap and that all the other notes are natural. Now we stack our next 3rds on top of those. The 3rd of F# is some kind of A. The 3rd of G is some sort of B. And so on... Again, make sure all the Cs and Fs are sharp. So the triads we end up with are: 1: D-F#-A (1-3-5) 2: E-G-B (1-b3-5) 3: F#-A-C (1-b3-b5) 4: G-B-D (1-3-5) 5: A-C#-E (1-3-5) 6: B-D-F# (1-b3-5) 7: C#-E-G (1-b3-b5) This pattern can be applied to the major scale in every key using these roman numerals: What do these mean? Well they're simply the numbers 1-7 in roman numeral form, each representing a scale degree. The capitol letters mean major chords, the lower case letters mean minor, and the chord with the 0 next to it is diminished. Let's apply this to the Fmajor scale: Diatonic harmony can get boring, so I'll teach you how to use other chords not in the key eventually, but first you have to have a solid grasp of diatonic hamony and how to create chords larger than triads. Homework: Watch ALL of the rest of the musictheory.net videos, starting on the category "Chords" and finishing on the final video of the category "Neapolitan Chords." Rates and Proportion Date: 7/11/96 at 8:10:9 From: Anonymous Subject: Rates and Proportion 10 men can build 2 boats in 4 days. Hom many men are needed to build 5 boats in 2 days? Date: 7/11/96 at 9:16:57 From: Doctor Patrick Subject: Re: Rates and Proportion First we need to figure out how long it takes a man to build a boat, and then we can apply that information to the question. If 10 men can build 2 boats in 4 days then the same 10 men should be able to build 1 boat in 2 days, since they would be building half as many boats in half as much time. Does this make sense? Then we can also say that if 10 men can build 1 boat in 2 days then 20 men should be able to build a boat in 1 day, since twice the number of men should be able to work twice as fast, right? This would also mean that 1 man would take 20 days to build 1 boat. Now we reverse the process and use this information to figure out how many men are needed to build 5 boats in two days. It would take one man 100 days to build five boats (20 days per boat*5 boats). If we want to cut this time down to 2 days we will need to increase the number of men by the same amount that we cut the time. Since the time is to be divided by 50 (100/50=2), we need to multiply the number of men by 50 also. Since 1 (the number of men who could build the boats in 100 days) times 50 = 50, it will take 50 men to build 5 boats in two days. -Doctor Patrick, The Math Forum Check out our web site! http://mathforum.org/dr.math/ Search the Dr. Math Library: Ask Dr. MathTM 1994-2013 The Math Forum Can you put it in the bank for mommy? You don't oh, uh, you know what else I'd like to buy? [8 more sec clacking with a clunk of blocks in the middle] Healing with Chakras: A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide ====================================================== Introduction ------------ Chakras are energy centers within the human body that help regulate all its processes, from organ function to the immune system and emotions. There are seven chakras positioned throughout your body, from the base of your spine to the crown of your head. Each chakra has its own color, vibration, and set of functions. If one or more of these energy centers become blocked or unbalanced, it can lead to physical illnesses and emotional issues. This guide will walk you through healing using chakras to restore balance and promote overall well-being. ### Key Tips and Guidelines * Before starting any form of energy work, ensure that you are in a quiet, comfortable space where you won't be disturbed. Turn off electronic devices and make sure the room is at a comfortable temperature. You may also want to light some candles or burn incense to create a calming atmosphere. * Practice deep breathing exercises before beginning to heal using chakras. Deep breaths help calm the mind and prepare the body for energy work. Breathe in deeply through your nose, filling up your lungs completely, then exhale slowly through your mouth. Repeat this process several times until you feel relaxed and centered. * Set an intention for your chakra healing session. What do you hope to accomplish? Are there specific imbalances or blockages you would like to address? Having a clear intention will help guide your practice and maximize its effectiveness. Healing Your Root Chakra (Muladhara) ------------------------------------ The root chakra is located at the base of the spine and is associated with feelings of safety, security, and grounding. Its color is red. Imbalances in the root chakra can manifest as anxiety disorders, fears, nightmares, problems in the colon, bladder, lower back, and legs, and disconnection from reality. 1. **Meditation:** Sit comfortably with your feet flat on the floor and your palms facing upward on your knees. Close your eyes and focus on your breath. Imagine a bright red light glowing at the base of your spine. With every inhalation, visualize the red light growing stronger, and with every exhalation, allow yourself to sink deeper into relaxation. Continue this meditation for 5-10 minutes. 2. **Affirmations:** After meditating, repeat positive affirmations related to the root chakra. Some examples include: * "I am safe." * "I trust the universe to provide for me." These video lectures of Professor Gilbert Strang teaching 18.06 were recorded in Fall 1999 and do not correspond precisely to the current edition of the textbook. However, this book is still the best reference for more information on the topics covered in each lecture. Instructor/speaker: Prof. Gilbert Strang OK. So this is the first lecture on eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and that's a big subject that will take up most of the rest of the course. It's, again, matrices are square and we're looking now for some special numbers, the eigenvalues, and some special vectors, the eigenvectors. And so this lecture is mostly about what are these numbers, and then the other lectures are about how do we use them, why do we want them. OK, so what's an eigenvector? Maybe I'll start with eigenvector. What's an eigenvector? So I have a matrix A. OK. What does a matrix do? It acts on vectors. It multiplies vectors x. So the way that matrix acts is in goes a vector x and out comes a vector Ax. It's like a function. With a function in calculus, in goes a number x, out comes f(x). Here in linear algebra we're up in more dimensions. In goes a vector x, out comes a vector Ax. And the vectors I'm specially interested in are the ones the come out in the same direction that they went in. That won't be typical. Most vectors, Ax is in -- points in some different direction. But there are certain vectors where Ax comes out parallel to x. And those are the eigenvectors. So Ax parallel to x. Those are the eigenvectors. And what do I mean by parallel? Oh, much easier to just state it in an equation. Ax is some multiple -- and everybody calls that multiple lambda -- of x. That's our big equation. Well I'm not sure if Cromer will manage or not. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV2/MT/jammt2.cha = = = = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Gilliam/TD/5m/46952wy.cha = = = Title: Understanding Slope Fields and Tangent Lines Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's learn about something cool called "slope fields." You might have learned earlier that a slope shows us how steep or shallow something is. In algebra, the slope of a line describes how steep it is. But when things get curvy, like hills or roller coasters, calculus comes to the rescue! It helps us find the slopes at every single point along those curves. Imagine having a special magnifying glass that lets you zoom into any tiny part of a curve. With this magical tool, you can draw a super short, straight line segment touching the curve at that spot - just like standing on a hill and looking along the ground at your feet. That miniature line is what we call a "tangent line," because it touches the curve at one point and moves in the same direction as the curve does. Now, imagine drawing lots of these teeny-tiny tangent lines across a whole hillside. By doing this, you create a fascinating pattern known as a "slope field." Each tiny line tells you the steepness of the hill at its own unique location. Together, all these lines give you a sense of what the hill looks like without actually seeing the entire thing. How cool is that? In real life, slope fields apply to many situations where change happens gradually over time, like population growth, water levels, or even weather patterns. They allow scientists and researchers to make predictions based on data points and trends. To practice creating your very own slope fields, follow these steps: 1. Draw a set of dots (or points) on a grid paper, equally spaced both horizontally and vertically. These will represent different locations. 2. Imagine a smooth curve passing through each dot. This curve will show you the slope, or steepness, at that specific location. 3. At each dot, draw a small line whose angle matches the imagined slope. Make sure to keep them short; otherwise, they may overlap with their neighbors! And there you have it - your own homemade slope field! Keep practicing until you feel comfortable visualizing how changing conditions affect various aspects of our world. Remember, math is everywhere around us, helping uncover mysteries big and small. Once upon a time, in a land far away, there was a big volcano. The volcano was very grumpy and would sometimes make a loud "boom" sound. People who lived near the volcano were scared and did not like the noise. One day, a brave girl named Lily decided to help the people. She thought the volcano was grumpy because it was hungry. She knew that the volcano liked to eat sour fruits. So, Lily went to the market to buy some sour fruits. She did not have much money, but she wanted to help. She gave her money to the man at the market and said, "I want to pay for the sour fruits, please." Lily took the sour fruits and climbed the big volcano. When she reached the top, she said, "Mr. Volcano, I brought you some sour fruits to eat. Please don't be grumpy anymore." The volcano ate the sour fruits and stopped making the loud "boom" sound. The people were very happy and thanked Lily for her help. From that day on, the volcano was not grumpy anymore, and everyone lived happily ever after. Do you want to go for a nap? Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's talk about something exciting in the world of numbers and logic - Satisfaction Classes! I promise this won't be like learning multiplication tables all over again; instead, think of it as solving a fun puzzle. Imagine having a special box filled with your favorite toys (numbers), and some magical rules about how these toys interact with each other. These magic rules make sure everything inside plays nicely together according to Peano Arithmetic - a set of basic rules governing our number system. Now imagine creating another secret club within this magical toy box by selecting specific toys based on their unique characteristics. We call these groups of toys "satisfaction classes." They follow additional cool guidelines known as Tarski's Compositional Clauses, which determine whether a group has enough different kinds of toys following certain patterns. But wait, there's more! What if we wanted to create a new rule using a brand-new symbol (*T*)? This mysterious star-symbol would represent "true" when applied to any toy in one of our exclusive satisfaction clubs. The whole bunch of rules now becomes a special theory named CT^-. Here comes the best part - even though adding extra superpowerful rules to CT^- makes it capable of doing things beyond its original powers, such as understanding complex reflections in arithmetic, just starting off with those basic compositional axioms keeps it humble. In fact, it remains just as powerful as good old Peano Arithmetic itself! However, once we allow limited advanced knowledge sharing among members of our clubs through carefully controlled communication channels (Delta_0 formulae), then voila! Our satisfaction class suddenly gains extraordinary abilities while still playing fairly within the realm of PA. How cool is that? So next time someone tells you math isn't fun or interesting, share this fantastic story about Satisfaction Classes, their magical toy boxes, and secret societies. Who knows, maybe you'll inspire future logicians! Is that a plate or a saucer? I'm just asking you why we can't keep on putting things away all day and even when we die. Title: How to Avoid Forgetting to Bring Money on a School Mall Trip Introduction: Memory lapses can happen to anyone, especially when preparing for an exciting event like a school mall trip. However, forgetting your money can lead to missing out on shopping opportunities or being stranded without funds for meals or emergencies. This comprehensive guide provides you with actionable steps and valuable insights into ensuring that you never forget your money during a school mall trip again. Step 1: Create a Checklist Rationale: A checklist serves as a visual reminder, reducing dependency on memory alone. It also allows you to add items progressively as they come up in conversation or thought, thus acting as a centralized organization tool. * Open a document (paper or digital) and title it 'School Mall Trip'. * List down all necessary items including clothes, snacks, water bottle, phone charger etc., followed by 'money' at the end of the list. * Review this list several times before leaving home. Key Tip: Place the checklist somewhere noticeable such as the refrigerator door or next to your keys so it becomes part of your departure routine. Step 2: Establish Preparation Time Rationale: Last-minute rushes increase stress levels which might cause oversight. By setting aside ample time to prepare, you reduce anxiety and improve focus. * Ideally, start packing the night before the scheduled mall visit. * Allocate sufficient time to review the checklist thoroughly while gathering required items. Guideline: Allow yourself at least one hour more than what you initially estimate needed. You may find additional tasks needing completion once started. Step 3: Designate a Specific Location for Your Money Rationale: Having a dedicated spot reduces confusion about where your wallet/purse is located, thereby minimizing chances of forgetfulness. * Choose a place within your bag where only your money pouch/wallet will fit. * Keep this area free from other belongings to avoid misplacing it. Key Tip: Using a brightly colored wallet or adding a unique tag makes identification easier. Step 4: Double-Check Before Leaving Home Rationale: As humans, we are prone to making mistakes; therefore, double-checking prevents us from overlooking essentials due to distraction. * Look carefully around your room and common areas for any forgotten item. * Confirm visually against your created checklist. Guideline: Engage family members in reminding you if possible, since fresh perspectives often catch things overlooked by ourselves. Step 5: Perform One Final Verification Upon Departure Rationale: The adrenaline rush prior to an anticipated event could still result in forgetfulness despite earlier checks. Thus, performing a final verification ensures nothing has been left behind unintentionally. * Verify the presence of your wallet/pouch containing cash/debit cards in your designated location. * Cross off 'money' from your checklist once confirmed. Key Tip: Make it a habit to always touch your wallet/pouch before stepping out to reinforce its inclusion in your preparations. Conclusion: By following these five simple yet effective steps, learners significantly decrease their likelihood of forgetting money during a school mall trip. Remember, consistency and mindful practice enhance our ability to form positive habits, leading ultimately towards greater self-reliance and independence. Happy Shopping! AbiWord is a free word processor that can be downloaded for free, it is open source and is much smaller in size than a proprietary alternative, Microsoft Word. It is available for FreeBSD, Linux, Microsoft Windows, Mac OS X, and QNX. Its mascot is Abi the ant. It is much smaller than many other commercial word-processors of its time, only using about 15Mb on a computer's hard drive under Microsoft Windows. [3 stressing second syllable. Title: How to Get Rid of House Flies ===================================== House flies are more than just a nuisance; they can also spread diseases such as salmonella and E. coli. Fortunately, there are many ways to get rid of house flies using simple household items or commercial products. This article will guide you through various methods to eliminate these pests from your home effectively. ### Method 1: Preventive Measures #### Step 1: Seal Entry Points Key Tip: Identify gaps around windows, doors, vents, and other openings that may allow flies entry into your home. Use caulk or weather stripping material to seal them up. This prevents new flies from entering and reduces the population over time. Guideline: Make sure all screens on windows and doors fit tightly without tears or holes. Keep exterior doors closed when possible. Regularly inspect your property for potential entry points and close them off promptly. #### Step 2: Eliminate Food Sources [picks up silver stick and puts it down through the hole in the center of the tower] "Then we will go to a country where it still exists, to the Orient, to Turkey," I said eagerly. Of course, flash cards can be boring. But if you get a little creative, they are cheap, portable, educational, and fun. Before your child knows the letter names or sounds: 1. Alphabet Hunt & Match 2. Memory/Concentration Game - My pack of cards from the dollar store included enough repeat cards to play a game of Memory. Be sure to name the letters for your child as they are flipped over. 3. Trace - Place tracing or tissue paper over the cards and have your child trace the letters. 4. Sort - Use the monkey, giraffe, chicken method to sort lower case letters. (Monkey letters have tails, giraffe letters are tall, and chicken letters are in between.) Or sort by round and straight. There's no wrong way - you just want them looking at and thinking about the letters. 5. Indoor or outdoor sound match 6. Make your own set of alphabet cards using index cards. 7. Decorate - we have these 8x10 alphabet cards hanging from a clip wire in our playroom, but they would look really cute framed too. When your child knows some or all the letter names and sounds: 8. Spread the cards all over the floor. Call out a letter and have your child jump on that letter. 9. Work together to arrange the cards in alphabetical order. 10. Match each card to an object that starts with that letter. 11. Slap Jack - play Slap Jack using the letters in your child's name as the Jacks. 12. Alliteration Game - each player takes turns choosing a card and listing as many words starting with that letter as they can think of in 15 seconds. Each word is one point. 13. Word Game - once they've mastered the alliteration game, move on to listing words that end with the letter. Once they've mastered that, try listing words with the letter in the middle. 14. Letter Pictionary - One player chooses a card without showing the other player. They draw something that starts with that letter while the other person tries to guess the object and letter. 15. Letter Charades - same as Letter Pictionary but acting out instead of drawing. For older kids: 16. Choose a topic and 10 cards. Think of a word related to the topic beginning with each letter from the cards. (Example: If the topic was volcanoes and I chose the cards L, A, and H, I'd say " L=lava, A=ashes, H=hot" etc.) This is a great way to review. I've done a similar activity with my students after finishing a novel. 17. Choose 5 cards and lay them out. Form a sentence in which each word begins with those letters. 18. Choose 1 card. Say a sentence that DOES NOT contain that letter. 19. Play Slap Jack. Vowels are the Jacks. 20. Choose a card. Write or tell a story with the letter as a main character. What's the personality of the letter? Quiet or loud? What does the letter like to do and what other letters does he/she hang out with? What ideas do you have for using alphabet flashcards? I'd love for you to share them in the comments. Well this is what the Wok's hats look. This one's so cute though and sad. Every week we will have new spelling words to learn. Practice your words everyday and in new ways. Here are the new words for this week and you can scroll down for some fun ideas to help you learn them: ABC Order - Write your words in alphabetical order. Then write them in reverse alphabetical order. Test Friday May 24- Our LAST spelling test!! Here are the words... Here are some fun ways to practice your spelling words. There will be 2 skill words this week. Make sure that your child understands the spelling pattern and is able to apply it to new words. Colorful Words - Use two different color pens to write your spelling words. Use one color to write the consonants and the other for the vowels. Do this one more time. Close your eyes and picture the word in your mind. Now try and write the word with just one color. Memory Game - Make pairs of word cards. Flip them over and try to match the pairs! Finger Tracing - Use your finger to spell out each of your words one letter at a time on your Mom or Dad's back. Then it's YOUR turn to feel and spell. Sing Them Loud, Sing Them Soft - Have your Mom or Dad sing the letters of a spelling word to you in a loud voice. You echo the spelling and then sing it again softly. Now BOTH of you sing the word in the voice you choose! Stairsteps - Write words as if they are stairs, adding one letter each time. (If you are using lined paper, begin up against the left margin line. If you are using graph paper, begin on the left.) Fancy Letters - Use old magazines, catalogs, or newspapers to cut out letters and glue them down to spell your words! Comic Strip - Use a handy blank comic strip form, like the one below, to create a comic strip using each of your spelling words! Plus, check out our the spelling game links in the reading and language arts page! But then everybody thinks he's six because he's late. Title: How to Get the Jack Russell Terrier Book on Nintendogs Introduction: In this article, we will provide you with an in-depth guide on obtaining the Jack Russell Terrier book within the popular Nintendo game, Nintendogs. This virtual pet simulator offers various breeds of dogs that players can train, play with, and care for, but some special items like books are hidden gems that add value and knowledge about specific dog breeds. The Jack Russell Terrier is one such breed; therefore, acquiring its dedicated book enhances your overall gaming experience. Let's begin! Step 1: Start Playing Nintendogs To embark on this journey, launch your copy of Nintendogs (any version) and start playing through either Story Mode or Free Play mode. Make sure you have sufficient time as patience pays off while searching for rare items like the Jack Russell Terrier book. Key Tip: Save often to prevent losing progress made towards finding the book. Step 2: Explore Your Surroundings Take your character out into different environments available in the game - parks, streets, beaches, etc., where other characters and their pets might appear. Interacting with these elements increases opportunities for discovering new things, including our sought-after book. Guideline: Keep exploring diverse locations until you stumble upon the book vendor. Step 3: Locate the Book Vendor The book vendor appears randomly throughout the game world. He carries several types of books related to dogs, although he doesn't always stock the Jack Russell Terrier book. If you don't see it immediately, don't worry - continue checking back regularly since his inventory changes over time. Key Point: Be persistent and patient when seeking out the elusive book vendor. Step 4: Browse Through the Available Books When you locate the book vendor, approach him and press 'A' to browse through his current selection. Look carefully at each title displayed on the screen, hoping to find the coveted Jack Russell Terrier book among them. Step 5: Purchase the Jack Russell Terrier Book If fortune smiles upon you and the Jack Russell Terrier book is present, buy it without hesitation using your accumulated funds from training and participating in competitions within the game. Once purchased, the book becomes part of your collection forever. Congratulations! You now own the Jack Russell Terrier book in Nintendogs. Use this resource wisely to learn more about this energetic breed and improve your skills as a caring digital pet owner. These systems contain 63 stars. 50 of these are red dwarfs, by far the most common type of star in the Milky Way. Much more massive stars, such as our own, make up the remaining 13. In addition to these "true" stars, scientists have identified 11 brown dwarfs (objects not quite massive enough to fuse hydrogen), and four white dwarfs. White dwarfs are extremely dense collapsed cores that remain after stars such as our Sun have exhausted all fusible hydrogen in their core and have slowly shed their outer layers. Despite the relative proximity of these 78 objects to Earth, only nine are bright enough in visible light to be visible to the naked eye from Earth, 6.5 apparent magnitude. All of these objects are currently moving in the Local Bubble, a region within the Orion-Cygnus Arm of the Milky Way. XXX she's going to get these to stand. did we did we left them at Maggie's house? Title: Get a Rocket Start in Mario Kart WII Introduction: Mario Kart Wii is a popular racing game that allows players to compete against computer-controlled racers or human opponents using customizable characters from the Mario franchise. One crucial aspect of the game is mastering the art of getting a rocket start at the beginning of each race. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth guide on how to perform this technique effectively. Step 1: Choose Your Character and Vehicle Select your preferred character and vehicle before starting the race. While some believe certain combinations offer better acceleration, there's no definitive proof that any particular combination provides a significant advantage over others when it comes to obtaining a rocket start. Therefore, choose based on personal preference or comfort. Step 2: Adjust Your Settings Before entering a race, ensure your settings are configured correctly. Go to 'Options,' then 'Controller Settings.' Make sure 'Tilt' is selected under 'Steering Control.' Also, turn off 'Auto Accelerate' as leaving it on can hinder your ability to control speed during takeoff. Step 3: Positioning Before the Countdown As soon as the loading screen finishes and you find yourself positioned at the starting line, position your kart slightly ahead of the grid markings but stay within the boundaries. Being too far forward might result in a penalty; being too close could slow down your initial acceleration. Step 4: Timing the Launch Pay attention to the countdown sequence (3-2-1-GO!). Press and hold the A button right after the second number appears (2) to build up your engine's revs. If timed perfectly, you should hear a distinct sound indicating maximum RPM has been reached just before the word "Go" is displayed. Key Tip: Practice makes perfect. The timing here is critical and may require several attempts to get used to. Step 5: Balancing Initial Movement Once the race begins, release the A button momentarily to allow your kart to move forward naturally without spinning its wheels. Then, immediately repress the A button to gain traction and begin moving rapidly. Releasing the A button briefly prevents wheel spin, which would otherwise waste time and power. Step 6: Maintaining Speed After achieving a successful launch, maintain steady pressure on the A button while gently steering through turns to preserve momentum. Avoid abrupt movements or excessive braking, both of which can reduce your overall speed. Guideline: Remember, smooth driving leads to faster times. Try to anticipate upcoming curves and adjust your trajectory accordingly rather than making drastic last-minute corrections. Conclusion: Mastering the rocket start takes patience and practice, but once mastered, it gives you a considerable edge over other players who haven't yet grasped this skill. Follow these steps closely, keep practicing, and enjoy outpacing your competitors in Mario Kart Wii races. This guide will walk you through the process step-by-step, providing detailed explanations, key tips, and guidelines to help you emerge victorious. **Step 1: Catch and Train Your Team** To defeat the Elite Four, you need a well-balanced team with diverse types and movesets. Aim for at least one Pok mon from each type category (Water, Fire, Grass, Electric, Psychic, Ice, Rock, Ground, Fighting, Ghost, Dragon, Dark) to ensure coverage against any opponent. Understanding their teams, strengths, weaknesses, and typical strategies can give you a strategic advantage. Here are the members of the Elite Four in Pok mon Black: 1. **Caitlin**: She specializes in Psychic-type Pok mon. Be prepared with strong Dark, Bug, or Ghost-types. 2. **Shauntal**: Her team consists mainly of Ghost-type Pok mon. Use Psychic or Ghost-type attacks, but avoid Normal and Fighting types. 3. **Grindor**: His team comprises Steel-type Pok mon. Focus on using Fire, Fighting, or Ground-type moves. 4. **Marshal**: He has a full squad of Fighting-type Pok mon. Bring along Flying, Psychic, Fairy, or other Fighting-type opponents. *Guideline*: Remember, dual types also play a crucial role here. For example, although Gengar is a Ghost-type, its Levitate ability makes it immune to Ground-type attacks, which are super effective against most Ghost-types. **Step 3: Plan Your Moveset** Ensure every member of your team knows STAB (Same Type Attack Bonus) moves - attacks of the same type as the user deal more damage. Also, equip your team with status-inflicting moves such as Sleep, Paralyze, Burn, Freeze, etc., to hinder enemy actions. Additionally, consider learning healing moves like Rest and Recover, and support moves like Light Screen, Reflect, Toxic Spikes, Stealth Rock, etc., based on your playing style. *Key Tip*: Always keep HM moves handy. They could save you during difficult situations. However, remember not to rely too heavily on these moves since they may limit your options due to move pool restrictions. **Step 4: Utilize Items Effectively** You want to put 'I'm in your pocket? Over in the Meadow illustrated by Ezra Jack Keats In a classic Appalachian counting rhyme, we visit a mother turtle, a mother fish, a mother bluebird, a mother muskrat, a mother honeybee, a mother crow, a mother cricket, a mother lizard, a mother frog, and a mother firefly. Each mother implores her babies to do what they do best: dig, swim, sing, dive, buzz, caw, chirp, bask, croak, and shine.Introducing the Story - Read the title of the book on the cover, pointing to the words Over in the Meadow as you say them. Repeat the title with the child. - Point to the pictures on the cover. Ask: What do you see? (Point out the three bluebirds in the tree.) What are they doing? (They are singing.) Reading the Story for the First Time - Read the rhyme, moving your finger under the words as you read. Give the child plenty of time to look at the pictures. Use your voice to highlight what the mother and baby animals say. The child may want to join you in this. Reading the Book Again and Again - Reread Over in the Meadow. Emphasize the words that rhyme and invite the child to fill them in as you reread. Give open-ended prompts on each page. For example, ask: What is happening in this picture? - Give prompts about objects or activities in the pictures. For example, ask: What is this animal? (This is a turtle.) Use your finger to point to what you are asking about. Evaluate the child's response. Expand it by giving more information. Ask the child to repeat the answer. If he or she needs help in answering a question, ask that question again the next time you read the book. Good words to ask about are listed in the vocabulary section below. Be sure to talk about objects and actions the child brings up, too. - You may wish to discuss the prompts shown below. - Use the words and the pictures to start the child talking about animals, the sounds they make, and where they live. You know what I think we ought do with all the stuff that we bought? var thetime=new Date(); As you can see, we are not assigning the new variable a direct value. Instead, the code above defines the variable as a new instance of the date object. This means we can use this variable in order to access the method functions of the date object. What are the method functions? Well, here a list of some of them: |Method Function||What the Function Does| |getHours()||Returns the current number of hours into the day: (0-23)| |getMinutes()||Returns the current number of minutes into the hour: (0-59)| |getSeconds()||Returns the current number of seconds into the minute: (0-59)| |getDay()||Returns the number of days into the week: (0-6)| |getMonth()||Returns the number of months into the year: (0-11)| |getYear()||Returns the number of years into the century: (0-99)| These are not all of the method functions, but they are enough to create a decent clock. Our example clock for this tutorial will only use the first three, but the rest my be useful to you to customize your clock. Now, suppose we want to get the number of hours into the day. We could do this by defining a variable and giving it the value of the getHours() method function. However, the following line will give you an error: Why? Well, remember that the getHours() function is a method function of the date object. In order to get that value for our variable, we have to use our instance of the date object we created earlier: var thetime=new Date(); This is part of object-oriented programming. Once we have created an object, we are able to access its member functions with what is called "the dot operator". By placing the dot between our object name (thetime) and its member function, we are able to execute the member function. In this case, the function simply returns a value (the number of hours into the day). We are using this value as the value of our new variable, nhours. We have used the dot operator in the past, when we used the document.write() function. The write() function is a member function of the document object, and the function writes text to the screen. An advantage of using objects is that objects can hold multiple values, where variables can only keep one value at a time. An object can also use any member functions it may have, like we did above. So, our date object named thetime can use all of the member functions. Now we can start getting the rest of the values for our clock. Let's get the hours, minutes, and seconds: var thetime=new Date(); See how we were able to use our object named thetime to grab three different values? It's almost fun, then again...yes, a bit more complicated than having a pre-defined object to use. With these values and a bit of extra coding, we can create a clock like the one below: Not bad, but now we must get into the extra coding we need to get the clock running smoothly. Remember, thetime.getHours() returns the number of hours into the day. This value is a number between 0 and 23. What can we do if we do not want a 24 hour clock, if we don't want 11:24 P.M. to be 23:24? To get around that, we need some code that will change any number over 12 back to 1,2,3,4, and so on. We can do this by subtracting 12 from our variable nhours when it has a value greater than 12: if (nhours>=13) nhours-=12; is shorthand for: Also, if the hour is 0, we know that is 12 A.M., so we need to make the zero into a 12. if (nhours==0) nhours=12; Now, when the number of hours is 14, the script changes it to 14-12=2. Just what we needed. We don't have the same problem for the minutes and seconds, but they present a problem of their own. Suppose the number of minutes into the hour is 32. This value is fine, the script will display a 32. However, what if the value is 2? The script would display something like this: What? Yes, we need a way to get a zero in there so the clock is readable. We want it to say 11:02. So now we need something to add in a zero before the 2 if the number of minutes (nmins) is less than 10. Let's try this: This code will add in the character 0 in front of the 2 if the number of minutes is less than ten. Another little problem out of the way, but we will also need to do this for the seconds: Had enough? Me too, but this clock still has another value we need to get. The clock displays A.M. or P.M. for morning and evening hours. This isn't to terrible. We can define another variable, let's call it AorP. We need the value to be "P.M." if the hour is greater than 12, and "A.M." otherwise. Here we go: One problem though. You need to place this section of code before our code that changes the clock to a twelve hour system. Otherwise, the hours will never get past 12, and it will always read A.M. Why? This is because when we change the clock to the twelve hour system, we change the value of nhours so that it is 0-12 in all cases. So, we must get our AorP value before we change the time system. Now, I'll give you the code for the script: You may have noticed we are accessing a form value and changing it in this line: So, you now know we need a form in the body of the document. Here is the one that goes with the script: <FORM name="clockform"> Current Time: <INPUT TYPE="text" name="clockspot" size="15"> </FORM> Also, you can see that we use the setTimeout() function to run our startclock() function again after one second. In this way, the clock will refresh every second so that you get a constantly running clock with the seconds ticking away. Of course, how do we get the thing started to begin with? The browser won't just run the startclock() function on its own. It needs some kind of event to happen. Well, how about when the page loads? Excellent choice, let's use the onLoad event. The onLoad command is used as an attribute in the body tag, much like defining a text color: I'm not sure I could handle that text color for long, but now we can see how the onLoad command will be put to work: Now, you can go make use of this clock if you need one. Otherwise, you have gotten a rather unorthodox introduction to objects and object-oriented programming. Well, maybe I'll add a more orthodox tutorial on the subject sometime, if I can stop saying the word orthodox. You know, I think the last half of this paragraph has been extremely unorthodox. [short silence, S sobs once, then cries again 435-38] Title: How to Get a Pokemon with Harvest and Skill Swap Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of obtaining a Pokemon with the abilities Harvest and Skill Swap. This can be useful for various game strategies, such as creating a Pokemon that can perpetually regain its used items or transfer skills between different species. We'll break down every step while providing essential tips and guidelines along the way. Let's dive right into it! Step 1: Choose Your Target Pokemon Firstly, select which Pokemon species you want to have both Harvest and Skill Swap abilities. Make sure your chosen Pokemon is capable of learning these two moves via leveling up, breeding, Move Tutors, TMs (Technical Machines), or HMs (Hidden Machines). Common choices include Porygon2, Breloom, Spiritomb, and Sawk; however, any compatible Pokemon may work depending on your preferences. Key Tip: Double-check if the selected Pokemon has an available move slot for Skill Swap since some evolutionary lines might lack space due to inherent moves. Step 2: Acquire Parent Pokemon with Desired Abilities Next, find parent Pokemons carrying either the Harvest ability or Skill Swap move naturally. For instance, Cherrim and Cherubi carry Harvest, whereas Breloom learns Skill Swap at level 48. If possible, try acquiring one male and one female so they can reproduce and pass their traits onto offspring. Otherwise, consider using Ditto (a genderless Pokemon) to breed instead. Guideline: Remember that only females can pass down abilities to their children directly. Males cannot do this but are still necessary to ensure genetic diversity among the offspring. Step 3: Breed Your Pokemon Breeding allows parents to transmit desirable traits like specific moves and abilities to their young ones. Place them in a Nintendo DS or 3DS Pokemon Nursery or Day Care facility. Keep swapping out the father until an egg appears containing the desired combination of moveset and ability. Important Note: The chances of hatching a Pokemon with the correct set of abilities depend on several factors including compatibility between parents, whether the mother holds an Everstone, etc. Patience is required during this stage. Step 4: Teach Skill Swap Through Various Methods Once you obtain the targeted Pokemon with the Harvest ability, teach it Skill Swap through methods mentioned earlier - leveling up, Move Tutor, TM, etc. Be aware that certain conditions must be met before teaching a Pokemon specific moves. For example, most TMs require purchasing from vendors within games after reaching particular points in the storyline. Meanwhile, Move Tutors usually appear post-game or demand special items as payment for services rendered. Step 5: Utilize Skill Swap Effectively Now that your Pokemon knows both Harvest and Skill Swap, utilize them strategically by switching abilities with another Pokemon mid-battle. By doing so, you enable the user to enjoy benefits associated with Harvest (regenerating Berries) even when battling against opponents who typically suppress item usage. Conclusion: Congratulations! You now possess a Pokemon equipped with both Harvest and Skill Swap abilities. Use this unique build wisely to enhance your gaming experience and employ unconventional tactics against formidable foes. Title: How to Get 240 Grenades Using the Grenade Launcher Glitch in Resident Evil Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through using the grenade launcher glitch in Resident Evil to obtain an unusually large number of grenades (240) for your inventory. This glitch works specifically with the GameCube version of Resident Evil (RE). Please note that exploiting game glitches can potentially ruin your gaming experience or cause unforeseen issues; proceed at your own risk. Additionally, this trick is intended for single-player offline mode only. Requirements: 1. A copy of Resident Evil for GameCube 2. A compatible GameCube controller 3. Save data from any point in the game where Jill has access to both the grenade launcher and the shotgun Step 1: Preparation Load your save file and make sure Jill has both the grenade launcher and the shotgun in her inventory. If she doesn't have either weapon yet, play the game until she obtains them. It is recommended to start from a later stage so that you don't need to replay most of the game just to perform this glitch. Step 2: Positioning Find a narrow corridor or hallway where Jill cannot move forward but can still turn around freely. Ideally, there should be no enemies nearby, as they might interrupt the process. The example used here is the room before the Tyrant encounter in the Training Facility basement. Step 3: Swapping Weapons Switch to the grenade launcher and ensure that it is selected as the active weapon in Jill's hands. Now, quickly press and hold the button for switching weapons while simultaneously tapping the button to fire the grenade launcher once. You want to switch to the shotgun immediately after firing a grenade round without actually seeing the animation for changing weapons. Step 4: Firing Shotgun Ammo While continuing to hold down the weapon swap button, rapidly tap the fire button for the shotgun several times. Since you are holding the weapon swap button, Jill won't shoot anything, but instead, she will attempt to equip and unequip the shotgun repeatedly. After about ten attempts, release the weapon swap button and then quickly press it again to resume normal operation. Step 5: Checking Your Inventory Pause the game and open your inventory screen. Navigate to the grenade launcher ammunition slot. Observe whether the number of rounds increased significantly-ideally reaching 240. If successful, congratulations! Proceed to Step 6. Otherwise, try repeating Steps 3-5 multiple times until you reach the desired amount. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Timing is crucial for executing this glitch correctly. Practice makes perfect, so keep trying even if it fails initially. * Performing this glitch too close to enemies may result in unwanted interactions between Jill and the creatures, hindering progress or causing unexpected behavior during the process. * While this tutorial focuses on obtaining 240 grenades, some players report being able to acquire upwards of 999 grenades using similar methods. Feel free to experiment further beyond the scope of this tutorial. * Remember that abusing glitches like these can sometimes lead to software instability or crashes. Always save your progress frequently when attempting tricks such as this one. Once upon a time, there was a persistent sock. It loved to slide down the slide! The sock was so happy when it was at the top of the slide, ready to have fun. It always slid down slowly and carefully, making sure it was safe. Bats are nocturnal animals. |How do they navigate, find food and find home?| By sound. Bats emit a high-pitched sound that bounces off objects. By listening to the returning echo, they can determine what the object is, how near it is, and whether it is moving. This is called echolocation. |What do most bat-species eat?| Insects. Most bats feed on insects, some types feed on fruit and there are a few species which eat other things. |What kind of animal is a bat?| A mammal. Of course bats are mammals! They have fur or hair and their young are fed on mother's milk. |Bats have very strong claws.What do they need them for?| To hang upside down in their roosts.. No it isn't said Mickey I found the gold at the top of this. Justified text is a block of text that is even on both the left and right sides. In standard English text, the right side of a block of text is ragged. This is called the rag. And the left side of the text block is lined up evenly along the left edge. If you want to justify the text, you want the left and right sides to be lined up evenly. This is called full justification. How Does Justification Work? The reason there is a ragged edge on one side of a block of text is because lines of text do not automatically end up the same length. Some have more or longer words while others have fewer or shorter words. So when a block of text is justified, the spaces that would otherwise have ended up in the rag need to be distributed through the line. Every web browser maker has its own algorithm for how to apply the extra spaces within a line. They look at word length, hyphenation, and other factors to determine where to put the spaces. This means that justified text is not going to look identical from one browser to the next. But browser support is good for justifying text with CSS. It works in IE 4+, Safari 1+, Chrome 1+, and Opera 3+. How to Justify Text Justifying text with CSS requires two things: a section of text to justify that is inside a block with a set width. That second bit is important. If the width on the container element is not set, justification will still work, but it will be more difficult to maintain because you won't know how much text you need to create a block of text to be justified. Once you have a block of text to justify, it's just a matter of setting the style to justified with the text-align style property. Here is an example of a block of justified text. When to Justify Text Justified text can be hard to read. When you justify text, lots of extra space is added between words on the line. And those inconsistent gaps makes the text a lot more difficult to read. This is especially important on web pages which can be more difficult to read because of monitor issues, lighting, resolution, or other hardware issues. Those spaces can line up with one another to create rivers of white space within the middle of your text. And on extremely short lines, justification can cause awkward lines that contain one word with extra spaces between the letters themselves. The only time that you can safely justify text is when the lines are long and the font size is small. There is no hard and fast number for the length of the line or the size of the text, use your best judgement. But remember that justifying text is relatively new in typography because it was so difficult to do. Once you've justified text, you should test it to make sure that it doesn't have a lot of rivers of white space. The easiest way to do this is to print out the text block (or the whole page), turn it upside down and squint at it. The rivers will stand out as blotches of white in an otherwise gray block of text. If you see rivers, you should make changes to the text or the width of the text block to get rid of them. My recommendation: only use justification after you've compared it to left-aligned text. You should justify text because you've chosen to justify the text, and because you have good reasons to. Don't justify text simply because you can. Okay now where are you going to put that? Title: How to Fill Out Money Orders Money orders are an easy and secure way to make payments when cash or personal checks aren't accepted. They work similarly to checks but are prepaid, reducing the risk of bounced payments. This tutorial will guide you through filling out money orders correctly and provide helpful tips along the way. **Things You'll Need:** 1. A completed purchase order or invoice (if applicable) 2. Valid identification (driver's license, passport, etc.) 3. The payee's name and address 4. A pen with blue or black ink 5. The money order itself ### **Step 1: Purchase the Money Order** Purchase your money order from a reputable source such as a post office, grocery store, or retailer like Walmart. Make sure that you have enough funds available before purchasing since they are prepaid. Keep your receipt for record-keeping purposes. ### **Step 2: Fill Out the Payee Information** Find the line labeled "Pay to" or something similar; this is where you enter the recipient's name. Use their full legal name if possible. Writing down the correct information ensures that the payment goes directly to the intended person or organization. Key Tips: * Double-check spelling and ensure accuracy. * Avoid using nicknames unless specifically instructed by the payee. ### **Step 3: Enter Your Address** There should be another section asking for your return address or purchaser's information. Providing this detail allows tracking of the money order if lost and provides proof of payment. It also enables the payee to contact you if necessary. Key Guidelines: * Always include your complete return address. * Be consistent with the name and address used during the transaction. ### **Step 4: Sign the Front of the Money Order** Sign the front portion of the money order, usually found near the top right corner. This signature indicates that you are indeed the one who has paid for and issued the money order. Without your signature, the money order isn't valid. Key Points: * Practice legible handwriting. * Only sign once - multiple signatures may raise suspicion or cause confusion. ### **Step 5: Optional: Add Memo Details** If there's a memo or notes field provided, consider adding relevant details about the purpose of the payment. For instance, write down an account number related to bills, invoices, or other transactions. Benefits: * Helps keep records organized. * Assists payees in applying payments accurately. ### **Step 6: Retain Copies & Records** Keep copies of both the receipt and fully filled money order until confirmation of successful payment processing. Having these documents can help resolve any disputes or discrepancies promptly. Advantages: * Easier dispute resolution. * Better financial management due to accurate records. By following these steps meticulously, you minimize errors and maximize security while making payments via money orders. Remember always to maintain clear communication channels with recipients regarding expected delivery times and methods. " Of course not [character voice]. LESSON 2: Helping Objective: Students will learn about how they can help people who have cancer. Time Required: One 40-minute class period Materials: Helping worksheet, Internet access LESSON STEPS1. Remind students that they have been learning about cancer. In order to review Lesson 1, have students go to different sides of the room based on their answers to this question: Do you think that you can catch cancer from someone? Reemphasize that cancer is not something that you can catch, that most people who get cancer are older, and that many people can live a long time with and after cancer. 2. Distribute Helping worksheet. Ask students, "How many of you have ever been sick enough to be lying in bed?" If you can, offer one of your own experiences. Ask students to think about what their family and friends did to help them feel better. Direct them to focus on the list of questions on the worksheet in order to help them get started on the activity. When the students have finished, review the answers as a class and discuss why they completed the worksheet the way that they did. 3. Ask students if they have ever done anything to make someone feel better or if they can think of things that they could do for someone who is sick. Highlight the topics discussed on the worksheet: - Talk to them - Give them special foods - Make them comfortable - Make them smile or laugh - Take their mind off feeling sick - Do something for them that they need help with or cannot do themselves 4. Have students role-play in pairs or small groups. One person should pretend that they are sick, and the other person can offer to do something to help the person feel better. The illness can be something that you can catch or something that you cannot catch. [picks up black base and moves it toward MOT and the yellow wand] Well you're just XXX go get it. Title: How to Hold a Golf Club Introduction: Golf is a sport that requires precision, accuracy, and proper technique. One of the most fundamental aspects of golfing is learning how to hold a golf club correctly. A good grip can significantly improve your swing and overall performance. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth explanation of how to hold a golf club, along with key tips and guidelines. By following these steps, you'll be able to develop a solid foundation for your golf game. Step 1: Choosing the Right Golf Grip Before you learn how to hold a golf club, you need to choose the right grip for your hands. There are three main types of grips available: overlapping (also known as Vardon), interlocking, and baseball grip. The choice depends on personal comfort and hand size. For beginners, we recommend starting with either the interlocking or overlapping grip, which offer better control and stability during swings. * Overlapping (Vardon) Grip: Place your left pinky finger over the index finger crevice of your right hand. This grip provides more control but may feel uncomfortable if you have smaller hands. * Interlocking Grip: Link your left pinky finger with the index finger of your right hand by wrapping them around each other. This grip offers improved stability while accommodating various hand sizes. Key Tip: Whichever grip you select, make sure both thumbs point down the shaft of the club. Step 2: Positioning Your Left Hand Place your left hand on the golf club first. Follow these steps to ensure correct positioning: 1. Locate the logo on the grip, typically found at the top of the handle. 2. Align the logo with the center of your left palm, pointing towards your target line. 3. Wrap your fingers around the grip, ensuring they cover two knuckles on the back of your left hand. 4. Keep your left thumb straight and pointed down the shaft. Guideline: Maintain light pressure when holding the club; avoid squeezing too tightly. Step 3: Placing Your Right Hand Now, add your right hand to the grip using these guidelines: 1. Rotate your right hand until the "lifeline" (the middle part between the thumb and forefinger) faces the left thumb. 2. Cover the left thumb with your right pinky finger, creating a secure connection without excessive force. 3. Adjust your right hand so that its lifeline wraps around the base of your left thumb. 4. Point your right thumb directly down the shaft, parallel to the left thumb. Tip: Try practicing this motion slowly to build muscle memory and confidence. Step 4: Checking Hand Alignment Once both hands are positioned properly, check their alignment by observing the "V" shape formed by the thumb and index finger of each hand. These "Vs" should point slightly inside the right shoulder, indicating a neutral grip position suitable for most players. If necessary, adjust your hand positions accordingly. Step 5: Fine-Tuning Your Grip Pressure Ensure that your grip pressure remains consistent throughout all strokes. Here's a simple test to help evaluate your grip pressure: 1. Pretend there's an egg under each armpit. 2. Swing the club back and through while maintaining gentle pressure, preventing eggs from cracking. By following these five steps and incorporating our key tips and guidelines, you're now equipped with the knowledge required to hold a golf club effectively. Practice regularly to strengthen your grip and enhance your overall golf skills. [begins to clear off objects from the black base] XXX going up in the forest someday.
